label: Methanol sourcing — spatial transport (carbon breakeven)
description: A spatial sourcing model (Layer 1a of the transport plan): methanol bought from three places — US Gulf (gas route), EU, and China (coal route) — and shipped to Korea by three transport-leg technologies, each carrying freight as opex, per-tonne transport CO2 as an emission, a per-source production burden, and an annual shipping capacity. The optimiser lands the cheapest methanol to meet Korea's demand. With no carbon price China wins (cheapest landed, but its coal-route methanol is the dirtiest at ~2.5 t CO2/t); a CO2 price above ~17 $/t penalises that production and flips sourcing to the US — the policy breakeven the model is built to surface. Annual resolution; in-transit time ignored. Sell the direction and breakeven, not the absolute cost.

label: Steel LCIA — cradle-to-gate (multi-impact)
description: A focused steel value chain for life-cycle impact assessment (LCIA), run under the Scenario simulator. Mining + coking (materials phase) feed a blast furnace + basic-oxygen furnace (manufacturing phase) to make 1 t of steel. Each process carries multi-gas FOREGROUND emissions (CO2, CH4, N2O, SO2, NOx, PM2.5); the purchased carriers (grid electricity, natural gas, coal, limestone) carry BACKGROUND cradle-to-gate factors, so the inventory spans cradle-to-gate and splits foreground vs background. An EF-3.1-style characterisation rolls the flows up into five impact categories — GWP, acidification (AP), marine eutrophication, particulate matter and photochemical ozone — reported per functional unit and by lifecycle phase. A green what-if variant (H2 direct reduction + electric-arc furnace) is compared against the blast-furnace baseline.

label: Green steel — AU/Qatar → Korea (cross-border)
description: A cross-border value chain: iron ore + green H2 (Australia), blue H2 (Qatar), and in Korea grid/renewable power, local green H2 (wind→electrolyser), an integrated steel mill, an automaker and a shipbuilder. Imported gas/coal/scrap and capped HBI are buyable. Each facility carries a MACC; the mill can transition iron-making (blast furnace → H2 direct reduction) and steel-making (BOF → EAF). A rising carbon price and declining CO2 cap drive the 2025→2050 transition. Solve jointly (system) or as a value chain (upstream → downstream).

label: Value chain — CCGT power + steel (KR)
description: A value chain (node hierarchy): a Power company owning a CCGT (a GROUP of GT + ST wired by steam) AND a Solar farm (a single component) supplying electricity to a Steel company's integrated mill. Solve jointly, per-company (value chain, upstream→downstream), or pick units.

label: Value chain demo — Korea (group/component)
description: A recursive group hierarchy: value chain → Korea Power / Korea Steel → facilities → machines (the mill runs a blast furnace, BOF and an EAF in parallel). Electricity couples the two companies. Open it in the Chain view to drill the levels; it solves jointly or per-company.

label: Steel transition — Korea (systempathway)
description: An integrated steel mill (BF-BOF baseline) that can switch to its alternatives — BF-BOF + CCS, H2 direct reduction (from 2030), or scrap EAF — as a rising carbon price (2025→2050) makes the coal route uneconomic. Fuels and feedstocks carry physical properties (calorific value, temperature, voltage); hydrogen gets cheaper and clean-grid electricity dearer over time; scrap is a capped import. Derived from PLANiT-Institute/systempathway.

label: Shipping — marine fuel transition (shipping_operator)
description: A ship runs one propulsion engine on a blend of marine fuels (IFO380 / bio-diesel / LNG / e-ammonia, each with calorific value and cryogenic-temperature properties and a tank-to-wake CO2 factor). The optimiser picks the mix within share bounds; the dirty-fuel cap declines and e-ammonia unlocks in 2030, so a rising carbon price shifts the blend to cleaner fuels over 2025→2050. Derived from PLANiT-Institute/shipping_operator.

label: Shipping carrier — fleet transition (KR ↔ AU/US/EU)
description: A single carrier runs ~100 ships on three trade lanes out of Korea — to Australia, the US and the EU — with a mix of fuels (HFO / LNG) and vintages, each cohort an annual-capacity asset (ships × throughput). From 2030 any cohort can re-engine to ammonia (zero tank-to-wake CO2) via a transition. Each counterpart prices the lane's emissions differently — EU highest (ETS + FuelEU), then US, AU lowest — modelled as region-tagged CO2 impacts priced over 2025→2050. The optimiser shows the policy balloon effect: the carrier decarbonises the high-price EU lane fully (ammonia by 2030), switches only the cheaper-to-convert LNG ships on the US lane, and leaves the lax-policy AU lane fossil. Cross-lane fleet reallocation (a shared pool split by route) is the deferred Layer-1b MILP — here each cohort serves its own lane. Sell the direction and the per-lane breakeven, not absolute cost.

label: Fleet reallocation — shared ship pool (KR ↔ AU/US/EU)
description: The Layer-1b demonstration: a carrier owns ONE shared pool of 85 interchangeable ships and decides each year how many to put on three lanes out of Korea (→ Australia, US, EU). The fleet is a TABLE — a pool count, not a box per vessel — and the optimiser reallocates ships across lanes as demand shifts. Total demand stays flat at 8 500 kt/yr but migrates from EU to AU over 2025→2050, so ships steam off the shrinking EU lane (35→15) and onto the growing AU lane (20→40) while the US lane holds at 30; the pool binds at 85 every year. Mechanism: an integer units[lane, year] with capacity-from-fleet (Σ throughput ≤ share·units, one ship = 100 kt/yr) and shared-pool conservation (Σ_lane units ≤ available). Per-lane CO2 is region-tagged and priced (EU > US > AU) for reporting; with one fuel and no abatement the prices don't change dispatch — the story is the reallocation. For the policy balloon effect and re-engining, see shipping_carrier.

label: Fleet distance — ships scale with route length (KR→AU/EU)
description: The Layer-1c demonstration: a carrier delivers the SAME 1,200 kt from Busan to a near market (Sydney) and a far market (Rotterdam, routed via Suez). Sea distances come from the searoute provider (Sydney ≈8,600 km, Rotterdam ≈20,200 km) and a ship's annual capacity falls with distance — fewer round trips — so the far lane needs twice the ships (8 vs 4) for identical demand. Any node carries lon/lat; a routes row gives each lane its endpoints + mode (sea via searoute, land via great-circle × a mode factor); capacity/ship = ship_size · operating_days / (2·distance/speed + turnaround). This is the physical-transport cost the value-chain optimiser sees — longer route ⇒ more carriers ⇒ more cost.

label: Fleet candidates — optimiser picks the fleet per lane (KR→AU/EU)
description: The Layer-1c+ demonstration: value-chain stream connections made PHYSICAL and carried by a fleet the optimiser CHOOSES from a candidate set. A Korean exporter ships one cargo to Australia (near, ≈8,600 km) and Europe (far via Suez, ≈20,200 km); each lane is a connection physicalised into a route, and the carrier owns three fleet types (HFO / LNG / ammonia) of which only SOME may run each lane — AU: HFO or LNG; EU: LNG or ammonia. Distance sets carriers needed (4 on the near lane, 9 on the far one for the same 2,000 kt) and fuel burned (efficiency × distance × cargo), priced at the fuel's price + the carbon price on its CO2. As the CO2 price climbs 30→200 $/t over 2025→2050 the optimiser switches fleets — HFO→LNG on the AU lane, LNG→ammonia on the EU lane — the 'which fleet on which lane, and when' decision. A connection with no fleet assigned stays virtual (teleport); a blocked corridor (Hormuz/Suez what-if) forces its flow to reroute or go undelivered.

label: Petrochemical MACC — ethylene NCC (petrochemical_macc_2025)
description: An exact port of the Korean Petrochemical MACC model (PLANiT-Institute/petrochemical_macc_2025), default-demand scenario. This is a greedy marginal-abatement runner, NOT an LP: it ranks abatement options (renewable PPA, industrial heat pump, electric naphtha cracker) by $/tCO2 and deploys the cheapest first each year against a net-zero-by-2050 emission target, carrying deployment forward irreversibly. Runs under the MACC (greedy abatement) optimisation method — chosen automatically when you open this example. Reproduces the source to floating-point precision (2050: 29.85 MtCO2 residual, $36.15 B cumulative CAPEX).
